items.text: Come my dear, let us make a desperate leap for liberty!
items.context: William Craft spoke these words to Ellen before they began their escape from slavery in Georgia on December 21, 1848.
items.publicReaction: This phrase became iconic in abolitionist circles and is widely cited in historical accounts of their escape. It encapsulated the desperate courage required for their journey.
items.source: William Craft, Running a Thousand Miles for Freedom; or, The Escape of William and Ellen Craft from Slavery (London: William Tweedie, 1860)

items.text: I think it is almost too much for us to undertake; however, I feel that God is on our side, and with his assistance, notwithstanding all the difficulties, we shall be able to succeed.
items.context: Ellen Craft's response when William proposed the escape plan using her light skin to disguise herself as a white male slaveholder.
items.publicReaction: This quote highlighted Ellen's agency and faith in their escape plan, demonstrating that their escape was a joint decision.
items.source: William Craft, Running a Thousand Miles for Freedom; or, The Escape of William and Ellen Craft from Slavery (London: William Tweedie, 1860)

items.text: It was not until we stepped ashore at Liverpool that we were free from every slavish fear.
items.context: William Craft describing the moment they arrived in England in December 1850 after fleeing the threats of the Fugitive Slave Act in Boston.
items.publicReaction: This statement became a defining moment cited in abolitionist narratives about the Crafts' journey to freedom.
items.source: William Craft, Running a Thousand Miles for Freedom; or, The Escape of William and Ellen Craft from Slavery (London: William Tweedie, 1860)

items.text: So I write these few lines merely to say that the statement is entirely unfounded, for I have never had the slightest inclination whatever of returning to bondage; and God forbid that I should ever be so false to liberty as to prefer slavery in its stead.
items.context: Ellen Craft published this letter in response to false rumors circulating in American newspapers claiming she wanted to return to her former enslaver while living in England.
items.publicReaction: The letter was widely circulated in both British and American antislavery newspapers, contradicting the false rumors and reaffirming her commitment to freedom.
items.source: Ellen Craft, letter published in Anti-Slavery Advocate, December 1852, and Massachusetts Spy

items.text: I had much rather starve in England, a free woman, than be a slave for the best man that ever breathed upon the American continent.
items.context: Ellen Craft's emphatic statement about preferring poverty and freedom to any condition of slavery, from her 1852 letter refuting rumors.
items.publicReaction: This powerful statement was quoted and republished in multiple newspapers and became a memorable expression of her absolute rejection of slavery.
items.source: Ellen Craft, letter published in Anti-Slavery Advocate and Massachusetts Spy, 1852

items.text: Since my escape from slavery, I have gotten much better in every respect than I could have possibly anticipated.
items.context: Ellen Craft's statement about her improved circumstances after gaining freedom, made in her 1852 letter to antislavery newspapers.
items.publicReaction: This testimony to the benefits of freedom was cited by abolitionists as evidence of slavery's degrading effects and freedom's elevating power.
items.source: Ellen Craft, letter published in Anti-Slavery Advocate, December 1852

items.text: The free air of good old England agrees so well with my wife and our dear little ones...it is not at all likely we shall return at present to the 'peculiar institution' of chains and stripes.
items.context: William Craft's reflective statement about settling permanently in England after fleeing the Fugitive Slave Act in America.
items.publicReaction: This quote demonstrated the Crafts' commitment to establishing a life of freedom in Britain and appeared in their published narrative.
items.source: William Craft, Running a Thousand Miles for Freedom; or, The Escape of William and Ellen Craft from Slavery (London: William Tweedie, 1860)

items.text: I must now give the account of our escape; but, before doing so, it may be well to quote a few passages from the fundamental laws of slavery; in order to give some idea of the legal as well as the social tyranny from which we fled.
items.context: William Craft's opening statement in the introduction/preface to their published memoir, establishing the framework for the narrative.
items.publicReaction: This statement set the book's methodical approach of documenting the legal and social foundations of slavery before describing their escape, which was praised for its scholarly rigor.
items.source: William Craft, Running a Thousand Miles for Freedom; or, The Escape of William and Ellen Craft from Slavery (London: William Tweedie, 1860)

items.text: Ellen Craft gave a very particular account of their escape...in so simple and artless a manner, as must have carried conviction to the mind of every one present.
items.context: Ellen Craft spoke to an audience of 800-900 people at a public abolitionist meeting in Newburyport, Massachusetts, describing their escape.
items.publicReaction: The Liberator newspaper reported this event as remarkable, noting both the unusual nature of a woman speaking to a large mixed-gender audience and the powerful impact of her testimony.
items.source: The Liberator, April 27, 1849; also cited in Documenting the American South

items.text: I think I can make a poultice and bind up my right hand in a sling, and with propriety ask the officers to register my name for me.
items.context: William Craft describing the escape plan where he would feign illness/injury to provide justification for not speaking and to avoid questions during their train journey.
items.publicReaction: This detail of their ingenious disguise plan was highlighted in antislavery literature as evidence of the Crafts' intelligence and strategic thinking.
items.source: William Craft, Running a Thousand Miles for Freedom; or, The Escape of William and Ellen Craft from Slavery (London: William Tweedie, 1860)

items.text: We knew it was unlawful for any public conveyance to take us as passengers, without our master's consent.
items.context: William Craft explaining the legal restrictions and dangers they faced while attempting to escape by public transportation.
items.publicReaction: This statement was cited to illustrate the comprehensive legal restrictions that slavery imposed and the courage required to defy them.
items.source: William Craft, Running a Thousand Miles for Freedom; or, The Escape of William and Ellen Craft from Slavery (London: William Tweedie, 1860)
